section.title: The Grief of God
section.sectionRationale: The world that began so good has filled with corruption. A mysterious boundary is crossed, human wickedness reaches its full height, and God looks on a heart that is only evil all the time. This tight unit moves from the spread of sin to the sorrow of God and His resolve to judge, and ends on a single hinge of hope: Noah found favor.
section.thesis: When human wickedness reaches its full flood, God is grieved to the heart and resolves to judge, yet one man finds favor, and grace survives the coming storm.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the boundary crossed and the limit set; the grief of God and the sentence of judgment; and the one bright exception, Noah's favor.

units.label: The boundary crossed
units.note: corruption spreads, a limit is set

verses.text: Now when men began to multiply on the face of the earth and daughters were born to them,
the {{sonsofgod|sons of God}} saw that the daughters of men were beautiful, and they took as wives whomever they chose.
So the LORD said, “My Spirit will not contend with man forever, for he is mortal; his days shall be 120 years.”

The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and afterward as well, when the sons of God had relations with the daughters of men. And they bore them children who became the mighty men of old, men of renown.

units.label: The grief of God
units.note: the heart of man, the heart of God

verses.text: Then the LORD saw that the wickedness of man was great upon the earth, and that every {{inclination|inclination}} of the thoughts of his heart was altogether evil all the time.
And the LORD {{regretted|regretted}} that He had made man on the earth, and He was {{grieved|grieved}} in His heart.
So the LORD said, “I will blot out man, whom I have created, from the face of the earth, every man and beast and crawling creature and bird of the air, for I am grieved that I have made them.”
Noah, however, found {{favor|favor}} in the eyes of the LORD.

sonsofgod.gloss: Literally sons of God. The phrase is debated: it may mean rebellious angelic beings, the godly line of Seth intermarrying with the ungodly, or powerful royal, tyrant figures. Elsewhere it can name angelic beings, but here it names those whose sin helps fill the earth with corruption before the flood.

inclination.gloss: A shaping, framing, or inclination, from the verb to form. Every yetser of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil continually. The word describes the bent of the heart, its settled direction; the same term recurs in 8:21, where God notes the heart is evil from youth yet promises not to curse the ground again.

regretted.gloss: To be sorry, to relent, to be moved to compassion or grief. The LORD regretted that He had made man. This is the same root as the comfort hoped for in Noah's name; here it expresses God's deep sorrow over sin, not a change in His character or a mistake in His plan.

grieved.gloss: To be pained, hurt, grieved. God was grieved in His heart, the very word used for the pain of the fall in 3:16. Human sin is no light thing to God; it wounds Him. This is a startling window into a God who feels the offense of evil deeply.

favor.gloss: Grace, favor, unmerited kindness. This is the first time the word grace appears in the Bible. Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD; the account will show Noah as righteous, yet the order here puts grace first, foreshadowing the gospel pattern of grace that precedes and produces a changed life.

basic.context: The world God made good has gone badly wrong. People multiply, but so does evil. A strange line is crossed by the mysterious sons of God, and human wickedness grows until every thought of the heart is evil all the time. God looks at all this with sorrow. The question is whether anyone is left who pleases Him.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, and it explains why the flood is coming. The hard question is who the sons of God are, and readers have long disagreed. But the main point is clear: sin has filled the earth, it grieves God's heart, and He resolves to judge, yet one man, Noah, finds grace.
basic.originalLanguages: Every inclination, the yetser, the bent of the human heart, was only evil all the time. God regretted, nacham, making man and was grieved, atsab, in His heart, the same word as the pain of the fall. Yet the passage ends on grace, chen, the first time that word appears in the Bible, resting on Noah.
basic.typology: Noah, the one man who finds grace and through whom the world is spared, is a picture of Christ, the one righteous man through whom a new world is saved. The grief of God over sin points ahead to the cross, where God bears the full weight of that grief Himself.
basic.christ: Noah finding favor in the eyes of the LORD points to Jesus, the truly favored and righteous One through whom God rescues a people from judgment. And God's heartbreak over sin is answered at the cross, where the grief of God meets the judgment of God in the death of His Son.
basic.god: This is a tender and sobering picture of God. He is not cold about evil; it grieves Him to the heart. He is holy and just, resolving to judge a world gone wholly corrupt. Yet even in His sorrow, grace appears, and He fixes His eyes with favor on one man.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 24 says the days of Noah picture the coming of the Son of Man. 1 Peter 3 and 2 Peter 2 recall Noah and the flood. Ephesians 4 warns us not to grieve the Holy Spirit, echoing God grieved here.
basic.oneStory: Sin, loosed in the fall and spread through the generations, now floods the whole earth and grieves the heart of God. Judgment is coming. But the story does not end in sorrow: grace lands on one man, Noah, and through him God will carry His purposes and His promise safely toward Christ.

context.literary: A hinge passage that closes the pre-flood world and opens the flood account. It moves from the multiplying of humanity to the multiplying of evil, mirrors the seeing and heart language of creation now turned to grief, and pivots on the single word however about Noah.
context.historical: For Israel this explained the moral cause of the flood, a world so corrupted that God had to act, while warning them that the same evil bent of heart lived in them and demanded vigilance and grace.
context.canonical: The setup for the flood, which the New Testament treats as a pattern of final judgment and salvation. The first appearance of the word grace here quietly announces the theme that will run to the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that human wickedness had reached its full measure, to reveal God's grieved and just response, and to mark Noah as the one on whom grace rested, setting up the flood as judgment and rescue.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the corruption and God's response; it does not prescribe the sin. Its abiding lessons are the seriousness of sin to God, the certainty of judgment, and the priority of grace.

debates.question: Who are the sons of God who took the daughters of men?

views.label: Fallen angelic beings
views.case: The phrase sons of God elsewhere refers to heavenly beings, as in Job, and the resulting Nephilim and the extremity of the judgment fit an unnatural, boundary-breaking union; some New Testament texts about angels who sinned are read in this light.

views.label: The godly line of Seth
views.case: The surrounding chapters contrast the line of Seth, who called on the LORD, with the line of Cain, so the sin is the covenant line abandoning its calling by marrying without regard to faith, filling the earth with corruption.

views.label: Powerful rulers or tyrants
views.case: In the ancient world kings were called sons of a god, and the language of taking whomever they chose and becoming mighty men of renown fits despots who seized women and exalted themselves, spreading violence.

debates.bearing: Interpreters differ, and each view is held by serious Christians; whichever is right, the passage's point stands, a boundary was violated, corruption filled the earth, and God moved to judge.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the passage is the human heart. Every yetser, every inclination or shaping, of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil, and that continually; sin is not an occasional lapse but the settled bent of the will. In response the LORD nacham, was sorry or relented, and atsab, was grieved, the same word used for the pain that entered creation at the fall, now felt by God Himself. Sin wounds Him. And then, against all that darkness, comes chen, grace, appearing for the first time in Scripture, resting on Noah. The order matters: grace comes before the account calls Noah righteous, the very shape of the gospel.

typology.type: Noah, the man who found grace
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the favored and righteous One through whom God saves a people out of a condemned world, 1 Peter 3:20-21.
The final judgment at the coming of the Son of Man, of which the days of Noah are the pattern, Matthew 24:37-39.

typology.type: The grief of God over sin
typology.fulfillment: Fully borne at the cross, where God takes the weight of human evil upon Himself in Christ.

study.christ: Noah found favor, grace, in the eyes of the LORD, and through this one man God preserves the world and the promised line. Here is a signpost to Jesus, the truly favored One, the beloved Son in whom the Father delights, through whom God rescues a people from a world under judgment; 1 Peter 3 explicitly reads Noah's salvation through water as a pattern of the salvation we have in Christ. Deeper still, the grief of God over sin, His heart pained by human evil, finds its answer at the cross, where that grief and that righteous judgment fall together upon the Son, so that grace might reach us as it reached Noah.
study.god: This passage opens a rare window onto God's heart. He is not indifferent to evil; He sees it fully and it grieves Him, wounds Him, to the core. He is holy and just, and a world given wholly over to wickedness cannot simply be left as it is; judgment is right and real. Yet God is gracious, and even as He resolves to judge, His eye rests with favor on Noah. The same God who is grieved by sin is the God who saves, and grace is His first and final word.

crossReferences.note: As in the days of Noah, people carried on unaware until judgment came; so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
God waited patiently in the days of Noah while the ark was built, and Noah's rescue through water prefigures salvation.
God did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah, a herald of righteousness.
Do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, echoing the God who was grieved in His heart here.

study.oneStory: The sin let loose in the garden and spread through the generations now floods the whole earth, and the God who made all things good is grieved to the heart. Judgment is coming on a world given over to evil. Yet the story turns on a single word, however: grace falls on Noah. Through him God will carry His purposes safely through the waters, keeping alive the line and the promise that run on to Christ, in whom the grief and the grace of God meet.

quiz.q: How does the passage describe the state of the human heart before the flood?

options.text: Every inclination of the thoughts of the heart was only evil all the time.
options.note: Sin had become the settled bent of the whole human heart.

options.text: The heart was a mix of good and evil in equal measure.
options.note: The text is starker; it says the inclination was only evil continually.

options.text: The heart was basically good but occasionally tempted.
options.note: This softens the passage, which describes wickedness as great and constant.

options.text: The heart had become perfectly pure again.
options.note: The opposite; the wickedness of man was great upon the earth.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the LORD regretted making man and was grieved in His heart?

options.text: God feels the offense of sin deeply, sorrowing over evil without changing His character or being mistaken.
options.note: The words reveal a God genuinely grieved by sin, not a God who erred.

options.text: God admitted He had made a mistake He could not foresee.
options.note: The language shows real sorrow, but Scripture does not present God as caught off guard or wrong.

options.text: God felt nothing and acted purely mechanically.
options.note: The text stresses the opposite; He was grieved in His heart.

options.text: God decided humanity was never worth making at all.
options.note: His grief coexists with grace, and He preserves Noah and the promise.

quiz.q: What is significant about the word used for Noah finding favor?

options.text: It is the first appearance of grace in the Bible, and it rests on Noah before he is called righteous.
options.note: Grace comes first, foreshadowing the gospel pattern.

options.text: It means Noah earned God's approval by his hard work.
options.note: The word is grace, unmerited favor, not earned reward, though Noah is later called righteous.

options.text: It simply means Noah was lucky.
options.note: It is not luck but God's gracious favor resting on Noah.

options.text: It means God favored Noah's whole city.
options.note: The favor rests on Noah, the one exception in a corrupt world.

quiz.q: How does Noah point forward to Christ in this passage?

options.text: As the one who finds grace and through whom the world is spared, he prefigures the righteous One who saves a people from judgment.
options.note: 1 Peter 3 reads Noah's rescue as a pattern of salvation in Christ.

options.text: Because Noah personally forgave everyone's sins.
options.note: Noah does not forgive sins; he foreshadows Christ as the favored, righteous rescuer.

options.text: Because Noah preached to angels.
options.note: That is not the point here; Noah pictures the one through whom God saves.

options.text: Noah has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament repeatedly links Noah's rescue to salvation in Christ.

quiz.q: Why is the identity of the sons of God best held with humility?

options.text: Serious interpreters differ among the angelic, Sethite, and ruler views, yet the passage's point is unchanged.
options.note: The main message stands whichever view is correct.

options.text: Because the Bible clearly names them and no debate exists.
options.note: The text is actually terse, and faithful readers have long disagreed.

options.text: Because the answer changes the entire meaning of salvation.
options.note: The gospel does not hinge on this detail; the point is corruption and coming judgment.

options.text: Because it proves the passage is only a myth.
options.note: Difficulty of a detail does not make the account untrue; it presents real history.

questions: The passage says sin had become the constant bent of the human heart. When you are honest, where do you notice your own heart leaning away from God by default rather than by rare accident?
God was genuinely grieved by human evil. How does it change your view of sin to realize it is not merely breaking rules but wounding the heart of the God who made you?
In a world gone wrong, grace came to rest on one man before anything is said about his goodness. Where do you need to hear that God's grace comes to you first, not as a reward you must earn?
Jesus said the days of Noah picture the world's readiness for His return. How awake or asleep is your heart to the reality that God will one day set everything right?
The whole world was corrupt, yet Noah walked differently. Where is God calling you to stand apart from the current of the crowd around you this week?

translationNotes.note: My Spirit will not contend with man forever can also be rendered My Spirit will not remain in man forever; the 120 years is read either as a new limit on the human lifespan or as a countdown of patience before the flood.

The LORD regretted, or was sorry, translates a word that elsewhere means to relent or be comforted; it expresses genuine divine grief over sin, not a confession that God made an error, since Scripture affirms He knows all things.
